original_abstract: Hydrogen produced with no greenhouse gas emissions is termed "green hydrogen" and will be essential to reaching decarbonization targets set forth by nearly every country as per the Paris Agreement. Proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) are expected to contribute substantially to the green hydrogen market. However, PEMWE market penetration is insignificant, accounting for less than a gigawatt of global capacity. Achieving substantive decarbonization via green hydrogen will require PEMWEs to reach capacities of hundreds of gigawatts by 2030. This paper serves as an overarching roadmap for cell-level improvements necessary for gigawatt-scale PEMWE deployment, with insights from three well-established hydrogen technology companies included. Analyses will be presented for economies of scale, renewable energy prices, government policies, accelerated stress tests, and component-specific improvements.
humanized_abstract: Green hydrogen is produced by using hydrogen from clean energy without any Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and almost every nation in the world is targets to reach decarbonization using Green hydrogen, as defined in the Paris Agreement. Proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers (PEMWEs) will also have a large share in the production of the green hydrogen. Even though the proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers have not yet gained a huge PEMWE market penetration. In fact, it is found that less than a gigawatt of global capacity of the proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers has been installed so far. In order to have a substantial role in decarbonization by producing the green hydrogen, it is required that proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers must produce hydrogen by expanding the production capacity up to hundreds of gigawatts by 2030 and more, as agreed upon in Paris Agreement as well. In this paper, the overarching requirements to do the enhancements of proton exchange membrane water electrolyzer at cell level for gigawatt-scale capacity will be provided in association with a study involving three green hydrogen heavy-weight technology providers in the industry. They comprised economies of scale and its implications on hydrogen prices using low-cost renewable energy prices. Impact of current governmental policies implemented at country-level to various levels and associated factors are also elaborated, as well as assessing accelerated stress tests, the effect of component specific improvements required at the cell level in the proton exchange membrane water electrolyzers to upgrade capacity up to few hundreds gigawatts.
